scope: user intentions for jit-agents (the agent abstraction: what the user wants jit-agents to be, do, own, and become)
method: claude-history read-only (NO sync); FTS5 + execute_sql against the SQLite history DB; only type=user, block_type=text rows from project workflowsPiExtension; verbatim user words extracted, agent-brief boilerplate excluded
queries_used: FTS: \"jit-agents\" OR \"jit agents\" OR \"jit agent\" OR \"jit-agent\"
FTS: \"agents as tools\" OR \"agent as a tool\" OR \"agents-as-tools\" OR \"agent as tool\"
FTS: \"north star\" OR \"north-star\" OR \"platonic ideal\" OR \"platonic\"
FTS: \"FEAT-004\" OR \"FEAT-005\" OR \"FEAT-006\" OR \"FEAT-007\" OR \"DEC-0044\" OR \"DEC-0047\" OR \"DEC-0014\"
FTS: oracle OR constitutional OR judiciary OR \"privileged agent\" OR ratif*
FTS: capabilit* OR sandbox* OR \"tool grant\" OR \"operation-granular\" OR perm*
FTS: \"composed from empty\" OR \"schema-shaped\" OR \"macro-rendered\" OR \"from empty\"
FTS: \"agents are agents\" OR \"workflow is workflow\" OR \"between a spec\" OR \"have a spec\"
FTS: (in-process OR subprocess OR in-session OR persona) AND (agent OR dispatch)
FTS: \"north star\" OR \"dispatch home\" OR \"agent layer\" OR dogfood*
FTS: attested OR attestation OR \"human in the loop\" OR \"human stays\" OR karpathy
execute_sql session-scoped scan of b62c055d (2026-05-24..25) for jit/agent/orchestrat/north/FEAT/DEC user messages
execute_sql full-body fetch of foundational truncated messages
caveats: Many fts hits on 'jit-agents' were LLM-composed agent briefs (long task prompts) — those are excluded as not user-intention. Verbatim quotes here are user-typed conceptual statements only.

Two quotes (JI-024, JI-028) are the user quoting/co-authoring a paste that begins with their own framing ('what is we add this thinking:' / the subagent-as-programmatic-action block) — they are user-authored intention even though prose-like; flagged in note.

Sparse/ambiguous themes noted in REPORT BACK: in-process-vs-subprocess dispatch mechanism is never stated by the user as an intention (only surfaces in debugging briefs); oracle-validation is implied via 'validate not fake validate' but the word 'oracle' is never the user's term.

intention: An 'agent' must be one consistent, uniform concept regardless of whether it is used as a monitor or used by workflows.
exact_quote: let's clarify some things. i want our concept of "agents" to be consistent and uniform. an "agent" is the same thing whether it be used as a  monitor or used in and by workflows.

intention: All existing agents are disposable proofs-of-concept; a correct generalized framework should be able to recreate any of them, so none need be kept.
exact_quote: all existing agents are development proofs of concept and attempts at building the thing we want to have. we need not keep any of them and if we do the framework correctly all can be recreated using the generalized framework we are aiming for.

intention: The jit-agents spec document is a living doc until explicitly frozen, and the uniform-agent definition must be present in it.
exact_quote: is that present in our jit-agents spec document. that document should be considered a living doc until we freeze it.

intention: The user wants a detailed spec file with clear outcomes defining what pi-jit-agents is aiming for.
exact_quote: do we have a detailed spec file and clear outcomes for what we are aiming for with the pi-jit-agents package

intention: Draft a jit-agents-spec.md from current knowledge as the working document to develop.
exact_quote: let's write a draft jit-agents-spec.md that we'll then work on, using what we do have and know

intention: pi-jit-agents is endorsed as the package name because it names the infrastructure for compiling, dispatching, and enforcing typed contracts on runtime-materialized agents — conditioned on it being architecturally clean.
exact_quote:
i don't mind that at all: pi-jit-agents as the package name. It says what it is: the infrastructure for compiling,         
  dispatching, and enforcing typed contracts on agents whose implementations are materialized at
  runtime from project state. Both pi-workflows and pi-behavior-monitors consume it to invoke JIT  
  agents — workflow steps for reasoning/analysis, monitor classify calls for behavioral
  classification.   -- as long as it is very architecturally clean and rational

intention: Frame jit-agents as 'jit agents as tools' — an agent is something people understand, but it can be composed (not static) and treated like a tool.
exact_quote: i like calling it jit agents as tools though. people think of agents; an agent can be composed not static and treated like a tool, as we're building towards here, no?

intention: The user adopts the thesis that a JIT agent is fundamentally a named callable unit with a typed input contract and a typed output contract — structurally identical to a tool call.
exact_quote:
The tool abstraction is fundamentally:
Named callable unit + typed input contract + typed output contract

That's it. JIT agents in pi-workflows satisfy exactly this:
•⁠  ⁠Named (⁠ .agent.yaml ⁠ spec)
•⁠  ⁠Typed input (⁠ inputSchema ⁠, validated at dispatch before any LLM call is made)
•⁠  ⁠Typed output (⁠ output.schema ⁠, validated on return)

From the orchestrating LLM's perspective, calling an agent step and calling a bash tool are structurally identical.
note: User pasted this as their own framing ('what is we add this thinking:'); user-authored intention, prose-form.

intention: What makes agents 'JIT' is that the interface (schema) is stable while the implementation is materialized at dispatch from current project state — state-aware tools, not a different tool per state.
exact_quote:
Traditional tools have static implementations. JIT agents have dynamic implementations — the prompt is compiled at dispatch time from current project state via ⁠ contextBlocks ⁠. The interface (schema) is stable; the implementation is context-parametric. This is analogous to JIT compilation: the contract is declared ahead of time, but the actual execution substance is materialized at runtime from current state.

So JIT agents are more precisely state-aware tools
note: Same paste as JI-008; user-authored framing.

intention: pi-jit-agents owns the full span 'between I have a spec and I have a typed result' — a clean architectural boundary extracted from pi-workflows and pi-behavior-monitors.
exact_quote: This package extracts shared JIT agent infrastructure from pi-workflows and pi-behavior-monitors into a clean architectural boundary. It owns everything between "I have a spec" and "I have a typed result."
note: From a user-authored task brief; the boundary phrasing 'everything between I have a spec and I have a typed result' is the user's own articulation of jit-agents ownership and is reused verbatim across later briefs.

intention: The jit-agents boundary IS a real architectural boundary — the user rejects the claim that it is not.
exact_quote: it is not true that the jit-agents boundary is not a real boundary.

intention: jit-agents is a wholesale refactoring whose disentanglement of what is extracted vs what remains in workflows/monitors has not yet been fully articulated and must be.
exact_quote: we do not have yet a list of the disclarities present in the code given that jit-agents is a wholesale refactoring. the distentanglement has not been completely articulated.

intention: Comprehensively articulate what in monitors and workflows is immutable so the elements to be extracted vs retained can be wholly segregated under the jit-agents package.
exact_quote: now let's articulate what in monitors and workflow are immutable, to wholly and comprehensively segregate the elements that will be extracted and what will remain in each, presuming the jit-agents package.

intention: There is one platonic-ideal vision (not several clean options); the user wants that single ideal identified and pursued.
exact_quote: i don't want 3 architecturally clean options if one is the platonic vision ideal and the others not. which is that?

intention: The first pi-jit-agents extraction should be a minimal, correct first enactment of the platonic ideal — not touching other packages yet.
exact_quote: plan mode for the minimal pi-jit-agents extraction design. i like that idea. it need not touch other packages yet, just be the correct first enactment of platonic ideal

intention: Treat the current articulation as not yet rational against the intended global outcome of the jit-agents vision — design must answer to that global vision.
exact_quote: the current articulation is not rational given the intended global outcome of the jit-agents vision.

intention: Project blocks are composable elements injected into jit-agents; the user wants blocks viewed in the context of jit-agents and workflows consuming them.
exact_quote: and from the perspective that blocks exist as composible elements injected into jit-agents? zooming back out to seeing project blocks in the context of pi-jit-agents and pi-workflows

intention: The governing lens for the whole project is an extensions-tools-constrained LLM that can act ONLY as a dispatcher of jit-agents, whose tools are limited to those the extensions provide.
exact_quote: the lens for our work is a extensions-tools-constrained llm who can act only as a dispatcher of jit-agents and whose tools only include those given in extensions. how does that influence our dogfooding perspective

intention: Substrate completeness is measured by whether a harness-confined LLM can dispatch a full arc, not by isolated tool/primitive existence (filed as the dogfood-completeness criterion under DEC-0014).
exact_quote:
This is also a meta-FGAP candidate: "substrate completeness measured by harness-confined-LLM
  arc-dispatch capability, not by isolated tool/primitive existence." Could file as FGAP-028              
  ("dogfood-completeness as the substrate-arc completion criterion under DEC-0014").
note: User affirming ('yes:') and directing the filing of this framing; the bracketed text is the criterion the user endorses.

intention: Evaluate whether the orchestrator-tasks-subagents pipeline currently used by hand is accomplishable by an extensions-tools-constrained pi agent using context + jit-agents.
exact_quote: evaluate the operational pipeline we've been using including orchestrator role tasking subagents and determine the extent to which it is accomplishable via an extensions tools constrained pi agent using context and jit-agents, taking into account known gaps and decisions in our context blocks.

intention: A subagent dispatch is an orchestrator using jit-agents directly — not necessarily a pi-workflow; the orchestrator can give a jit-agent a tool (a specific bash command, a file read, a file write) in its rendered prompt.
exact_quote: let's presume it wasn't necessarily a pi-workflow; rather the orchestrator using jit-agents (which could be tasked bash and given it as a tool in its rendered prompt, e.g., or a file read, or a file write.
note: Opening of the constitutional-model paste; user-authored.

intention: A subagent is a programmatic action — a tool call by the main-context agent that carries relevant atomic context chunks composed into the shape of a prompt.
exact_quote:
A subagent is a programmatic action. 

A subagent is a tool call by the main context agent that carries relevant atomic context chunks composed into the shape of a prompt.
note: User-authored paste; refines JI-007/JI-008 into the agents-as-tools/FEAT-004 statement.

intention: Each subagent gets its own tools; specific tool calls can be permitted exactly for specific tasks and no others (e.g. one named bash command, not bash generally) — capabilities are atomically, just-in-time composed, possibly from a registry, possibly sandboxed.
exact_quote:
The sub agent gets its own tools to call. 

Specific sub agent tool calls can be permed exactly for specific tasks in the prompt and no others. Eg A bash command. Not bash. 

Tools and perms can be atomically and just-in-time composed, too. There can be a registry of them. 

Could even likely be sandboxed.
note: User-authored paste; the operation-granular capability-grant intention (FEAT-005 / FGAP-099).

intention: A jit-agent starts from empty — no perms, no tools — and configs, schemas, and macros bring everything into existence from that empty state.
exact_quote: No perms. No tools. Configs and schemas and macros bring all into existence from empty state.

intention: The human stays in the loop by authorizing the config and its mutations by the main-context agent — a constitutional separation of legislative/executive/judicial.
exact_quote:
The human stays in the loop by authorizing the config and its mutations by the main context agent. 

Constitutional, so to speak. Haha. Legislative, executive, judicial.
note: User-authored paste; origin of DEC-0047 constitutional model.

intention: The ideal end-state: a constrained-harness LLM orchestrator writes blocks that are fed to a privileged jit-agent, so the agent can cause validated changes to the code from within the constraint.
exact_quote: the ideal state we will get to is that within the constrained harness the llm orchestrator can write blocks as necessary that will be fed to a jit-agent that does have perms; such that the agent within the constraint can cause validated changes to be made to the code. do we have that as gaps? we can think it through.
note: The FEAT-006 north-star loop: constrained orchestrator -> spec/block -> privileged agent -> validated change.

intention: The agent must have the capability to genuinely validate, not fake-validate — the same real validation capability the human orchestrator has.
exact_quote: essentially we need the capability that you as orchestrator here have to validate not fake validate. reflect on that.
note: Origin of FGAP-102 (oracle-not-LLM validation); user never uses the word 'oracle'.

intention: Tools are explicitly granted to jit-agents via --tools; schemas, macros, and templates are what the agent programmatically carries per what is declared.
exact_quote: think of it that we explicity grant tools using --tools for jit agents: schemas and macros and templates such that the agent gets programmatically carry what's declared

intention: The validity/currency-deciding tool is simply a jit-agent granted specific perms (read, amend blocks); the primitives for this likely already exist.
exact_quote: our tool for deciding valid + current is simply the work of a jit agent with perms for specific things -- read, amend blocks, etc. i think we have the primitives.

intention: Flipping content to valid+current is a human-in-the-loop act: the human's okay, based on what the agent proffers, is the authority for the flip — not yet finalized, verging into Karpathy LLM-wiki space.
exact_quote: that's a human in the loop thing: the human's okay is the authority of the flip, based on what the agent profers. that's not finalized yet, so note it needs more thinking. we're verging into karpathy llm wiki space with that.

intention: Only the main-context orchestrator may author an agent's spec/schema; no agent authors itself, and the other agents never even know the spec exists.
exact_quote: no agent would author itself. the only agent that can author spec/schema is main context orchestrator. the others never even know the thing exists.

intention: DEC-0047 settled framing: subagent tools are composed per-invocation from its spec, default empty; the orchestrator is the sole spec-author; capability-widening spec/schema writes are human-ratified.
exact_quote:
DEC-0047 (open — to settle) — capability model: subagent tools composed per-invocation from
  its spec, default empty; orchestrator is the sole spec-author; capability-widening spec/schema
   writes are human-ratified.
note: Consolidates JI-023/JI-024/JI-025/JI-031 into the DEC-0047 capability model the user names to settle.

intention: Skills should be JIT too — schema-shaped, macro'd, reusable/composable on the fly — the same treatment as agents.
exact_quote: i'm beginning to think there's no reason skills shouldn't be jit too; schema shaped; macro'd; reusable or composible on the fly

intention: Everything — substrate, agents, capabilities, guidance — is schema-shaped data, macro-rendered, composed from empty to need; a candidate to persist as a context block.
exact_quote:
"everything — substrate, agents, 
  capabilities, guidance — is schema-shaped data, macro-rendered, composed from empty to need."
note: The unifying system thesis; generalizes JI-024.

intention: Agent logic belongs in jit-agents, not in workflows; the two must be detangled — 'workflow is workflow; jit-agents are agents' — and expediency that persists agents-in-workflows is rejected.
exact_quote: expediency is never a valid consideration, especially when it persists something we're intentionally moving away from. no filing yet. detangle the issues. workflow is workflow. jit-agents are agents.
note: The DEC-0044 detangle directive; reaffirms JI-001/JI-010 — agent abstraction must live in jit-agents as its home, not be re-implemented in workflows.

intention: Agent fixes must be implemented at the correct site (jit-agents) and removed from the wrong site (workflows); implementing in workflows was a mis-sited expedient fix.
exact_quote: now though we've doubled the work: implementing what should have been implemented at the correct site, jit-agents, and removing it from the wrong side, workflows.

intention: pi-workflows should not be doing anything with agent schemas and templates — that surface belongs to jit-agents.
exact_quote: why is pi-workflows doing anything with agent schemas and templates?

intention: Settle DEC-0044 (the dispatch home for agents) by exploring jit-agents in plan mode.
exact_quote: plan mode, explore jit-agents, settle DEC-0044

intention: The user probes whether workflow is effectively doing the work of jit-agents — surfacing the overlap to be resolved in favor of jit-agents owning the agent abstraction.
exact_quote: a question though: isn't workflow doing the work of jit-agents then?
